title: NVIDIA Blackwell Architecture: A Deep Dive into the Next Generation of AI Computing | by Nagesh Vishnumurthy | Medium
text:
NVIDIA Blackwell Architecture: A Deep Dive into the Next Generation of AI Computing
NVIDIA’s Blackwell architecture represents a monumental leap forward in GPU design, succeeding both the Hopper and Ada Lovelace microarchitectures. Released in 2024, Blackwell is designed to power the next generation of AI training and inference workloads, featuring 208 billion transistors manufactured using TSMC’s custom 4NP process. This deep dive explores the architectural innovations that make Blackwell the most powerful AI accelerator ever created.
Overview and Dual-Die Architecture
The defining characteristic of Blackwell is its revolutionary dual-die design. Unlike traditional monolithic GPU architectures, Blackwell features two reticle-limited dies connected by a 10 TB/s chip-to-chip interconnect in a unified single GPU package. This innovative approach allows NVIDIA to circumvent the physical limitations of photolithography reticle sizes while maintaining full cache coherency across both dies.
The dual-die configuration creates what is effectively the world’s largest GPU, with each die contributing to a unified compute platform. The 10 TB/s interconnect ensures that the two dies operate as a single coherent unit, with no memory locality issues or performance penalties typically associated with multi-chip modules.
Product Lineup and Specifications
The Blackwell family includes several variants targeting different market segments:
B100 (Data Center)
• Dual-die GB100 design with 208 billion transistors
• 192 GB HBM3e memory with up to 8 TB/s bandwidth
• 5th-gen NVLink delivering 1.8 TB/s per GPU
• 700W TDP
• Optimized for balanced compute and memory performance
B200 (High-Performance Data Center)
• Same dual-die GB100 architecture
• 192 GB HBM3e memory with up to 8 TB/s bandwidth
• Enhanced tensor performance with higher peak FLOPS
• 1000W TDP
• 2x performance improvement over H200 in TF32, FP16, and FP8
Blackwell Ultra (Next-Gen Flagship)
• Mid-cycle architectural boost
• 288 GB HBM3e memory (50% increase)
• 1400W TDP
• Enhanced transformer engine with 2x attention-layer acceleration
• 1.5x more AI compute FLOPS compared to standard Blackwell
GB200 Grace Blackwell Superchip
• Combines two B200 Tensor Core GPUs with NVIDIA Grace CPU
• 900 GB/s NVLink-C2C chip-to-chip interconnect
• Fully memory-coherent unified architecture
• 384 GB total HBM3e memory with 16 TB/s bandwidth
• ARM Neoverse V2 cores (72 cores) with multilevel cache hierarchy
GB200 NVL72
• Rack-scale liquid-cooled system
• 36 Grace CPUs and 72 Blackwell GPUs
• 72-GPU NVLink domain acting as single massive GPU
• 130 TB/s total NVLink bandwidth
• Up to 576 TB/s aggregate bandwidth in multi-rack configurations
Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) Architecture
The Blackwell SM represents the fundamental compute building block of the architecture. Each Blackwell GPU contains 148 Streaming Multiprocessors, a 12% increase over Hopper’s 132 SMs.
SM Core Components:
• 128 CUDA Cores per SM (unified INT32/FP32 execution units)
• 4 Fifth-Generation Tensor Cores per SM
• 1 Fourth-Generation Ray Tracing (RT) Core per SM
• 4 Texture Units per SM
• 256 KB Register File per SM
• 228 KB combined Shared Memory + L1 Cache per SM (configurable)
• 256 KB Tensor Memory (TMEM) per SM
• 65,536 registers per SM
The most significant architectural change in the Blackwell SM is the introduction of Tensor Memory (TMEM), a specialized memory structure designed specifically for Tensor Core operations. This addresses the extreme register pressure that plagued Hopper’s tensor operations.
Tensor Memory (TMEM) Innovation
Tensor Memory is arguably the most transformative addition to Blackwell’s SM architecture. TMEM is organized as a 2D structure with 128 rows (lanes) and 512 columns of 4-byte cells, totaling 256 KB per SM — matching the size of the register file itself.
Why TMEM Matters:
In previous architectures including Hopper, tensor operations suffered from extreme register pressure. Matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) operations required loading matrix fragments from shared memory into thread registers, executing the operation, and storing results back. This created several bottlenecks:
1. Register File Contention: Large matrix operations consumed massive amounts of register space, limiting occupancy and the number of in-flight operations
2. Memory Traffic: Constant data movement between shared memory and registers created bandwidth bottlenecks
3. Synchronization Overhead: Multiple threads had to synchronize at every step of the computation
TMEM solves these issues by providing a dedicated staging area for tensor operations that sits closer to the Tensor Cores than traditional shared memory. Key advantages include:
• Direct Tensor Core Access: TMEM is tightly coupled with the Tensor Cores, reducing latency and eliminating register file pressure
• Higher Throughput: Dedicated pathways allow sustained tensor throughput without competing for general-purpose resources
• Dual-Thread-Block MMA: Paired SMs can cooperate on single MMA operations, sharing operands through TMEM and reducing redundant memory traffic
The result is a measured 50x performance improvement in optimized GEMM (General Matrix Multiply) kernels when fully leveraging TMEM compared to traditional register-based approaches.
Fifth-Generation Tensor Cores
Blackwell’s Tensor Cores represent the fifth generation of NVIDIA’s matrix acceleration technology. Each SM contains 4 Tensor Cores, with 592 total across the full GB100 die.
Tensor Core Performance (per GPU):
• FP4 Tensor Core: 20 petaFLOPS (B200) / 18 petaFLOPS (B100)
• FP6/FP8 Tensor Core: 10 petaFLOPS
• FP16 Tensor Core: 5 petaFLOPS
• TF32 Tensor Core: 2.5 petaFLOPS
• FP64 Tensor Core: 45 teraFLOPS (B200) / 40 teraFLOPS (B100)
The introduction of FP4 and FP6 precision formats is revolutionary. These ultra-low precision formats enable massive model sizes to fit in memory while maintaining acceptable accuracy through the enhanced Transformer Engine.
Transformer Engine with Micro-Tensor Scaling:
The second-generation Transformer Engine introduces micro-tensor scaling, a fine-grain quantization technique that dynamically adjusts precision at the sub-tensor level. This enables:
• 4-bit Floating Point (FP4) AI: Doubles the performance and model size that memory can support
• Dynamic Range Preservation: Fine-grain scaling maintains accuracy even at extreme quantization levels
• 2x Attention Layer Acceleration: Blackwell Ultra variant provides additional speedup for the attention mechanism critical to transformer models
Compared to Hopper, Blackwell achieves:
• 2x higher throughput in FP8 operations
• 4x improvement in FP4 operations (new capability)
• 30x speedup for massive models (trillion+ parameters)
Memory Hierarchy and Bandwidth
Blackwell’s memory subsystem has been extensively optimized to feed the massive compute capabilities of the tensor cores and CUDA cores.
Memory Architecture:
L1/Shared Memory: 228 KB per SM (configurable split)
• 19% increase from Hopper’s 192 KB
• Configurable as pure L1, pure shared memory, or hybrid
• Critical for staging data before TMEM operations
L2 Cache: 192 MB shared across all SMs
• 4x increase over Hopper’s 50 MB
• 3.8x increase over Ampere’s 40 MB
• Massive L2 reduces HBM pressure and improves multi-SM data sharing
HBM3e Memory:
• B100/B200: 192 GB with 8 TB/s bandwidth
• Blackwell Ultra: 288 GB with enhanced bandwidth
• 67% higher bandwidth than Hopper H100 (4.8 TB/s)
• Lower latency than previous HBM generations
The dramatic L2 cache increase is particularly significant. In AI workloads, the same weights are often accessed repeatedly across different batches. The 192 MB L2 can cache substantial portions of model weights, dramatically reducing HBM traffic.
Memory Bandwidth Hierarchy Analysis:
Let’s examine the memory bandwidth at different levels (measured on B200):
1. TMEM Bandwidth: ~100 TB/s effective (direct tensor core coupling)
2. L1/Shared Memory: ~40 TB/s aggregate across all SMs
3. L2 Cache Bandwidth: ~20 TB/s
4. HBM3e Bandwidth: 8 TB/s
This hierarchy demonstrates NVIDIA’s strategy: keep data as close to compute as possible. TMEM provides 12.5x more bandwidth than HBM, explaining the dramatic performance gains in optimized code.
NVLink 5.0 and Interconnect Architecture
Blackwell introduces the fifth generation of NVLink, NVIDIA’s proprietary high-speed GPU-to-GPU interconnect.
NVLink 5.0 Specifications:
• 100 GT/s transfer rate per link (doubled from NVLink 4.0)
• 50 GB/s bandwidth per link (bidirectional)
• 18 links per GPU
• 1.8 TB/s total bandwidth per GPU
• PAM4 differential-pair encoding
• Support for up to 576 GPUs in a single domain
Compared to Previous Generations:
• NVLink 4.0 (Hopper): 900 GB/s per GPU
• NVLink 3.0 (Ampere): 600 GB/s per GPU
• NVLink 2.0 (Volta): 300 GB/s per GPU
• PCIe Gen 6.0: 242 GB/s (x16 slot)
Blackwell’s NVLink 5.0 provides 7.4x more bandwidth than PCIe Gen 6.0, making it essential for multi-GPU training and inference workloads.
NVLink-C2C (Chip-to-Chip):
The GB200 Superchip uses a specialized variant called NVLink-C2C to connect the Grace CPU with Blackwell GPUs:
• 900 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth
• Fully memory-coherent operation
• 25x more energy efficient than PCIe Gen 5
• 90x more area-efficient than PCIe Gen 5 PHY
NVLink Switch Architecture:
The fifth-generation NVLink Switch enables full-mesh connectivity in large GPU clusters:
• 64 ports per switch
• 7.2 TB/s aggregate bandwidth per switch
• Non-blocking all-to-all communication
• Support for 72-GPU NVLink domains (GB200 NVL72)
• Scalable to 576 GPUs across multiple racks
The GB200 NVL72 configuration demonstrates the power of this architecture: 36 Grace CPUs and 72 Blackwell GPUs connected through NVLink switches create a 130 TB/s GPU bandwidth domain that operates as a single massive GPU for large model training.
Decompression Engine and Data Processing
Blackwell includes dedicated hardware decompression engines designed to accelerate data preprocessing:
• Hardware-accelerated decompression for compressed datasets
• Support for common compression formats (gzip, LZ4, etc.)
• Offloads CPU from decompression tasks
• Enables higher effective I/O bandwidth by storing data compressed
• Critical for large-scale training where dataset size exceeds storage bandwidth
The decompression engine works in concert with the GB200’s Grace CPU, which can decompress data on-the-fly as it streams to the GPUs, effectively multiplying storage bandwidth.
Multimedia Decoders:
Blackwell also integrates enhanced multimedia decoders for video and image processing:
• AV1 decode support
• H.264/H.265 decode acceleration
• JPEG decode acceleration
• Multi-stream decode capability
These decoders are particularly useful for computer vision workloads where large video datasets need to be processed in real-time.
Power Efficiency and Thermal Design
Despite massive performance increases, Blackwell maintains reasonable power envelopes through several innovations:
Power Management Features:
• TSMC 4NP process node optimization
• Fine-grain power gating per SM
• Dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)
• Clock gating for idle units
• Optimized data movement to reduce unnecessary traffic
Thermal Solutions:
B100/B200: Air-cooled with enhanced cooling architecture
• Advanced vapor chamber design
• Optimized fin stack geometry
• High-performance thermal interface materials
GB200 NVL72: Liquid-cooled rack-scale design
• Direct liquid cooling to GPU dies
• Reduced datacenter cooling requirements
• Higher density deployments possible
• Lower total cost of ownership (TCO)
Performance per Watt:
Compared to Hopper H100:
• 2.5x performance per watt in FP8 training workloads
• 3x performance per watt in FP4 inference workloads
• Lower idle power consumption
• Better power efficiency at partial utilization
The combination of process node improvements, architectural optimizations, and advanced cooling enables Blackwell to deliver unprecedented compute density.
Architectural Comparison: Blackwell vs Hopper vs Ampere
To understand Blackwell’s improvements, let’s examine key architectural metrics across three generations:
[Architectural Comparison Table]
| Feature | Ampere (A100) | Hopper (H100) | Blackwell (B200) |
| — — — — -| — — — — — — — -| — — — — — — — -| — — — — — — — — — |
| Process Node | 7nm | 4nm | 4NP (custom) |
| Transistors | 54B | 80B | 208B |
| Die Configuration | Single | Single | Dual-die |
| SMs | 108 | 132 | 148 |
| CUDA Cores/SM | 64 | 128 | 128 |
| Tensor Cores/SM | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| Register File | 256 KB | 256 KB | 256 KB |
| TMEM | — | — | 256 KB |
| L1/Shared Memory | 192 KB | 192 KB | 228 KB |
| L2 Cache | 40 MB | 50 MB | 192 MB |
| HBM Memory | 80 GB | 80 GB | 192 GB |
| Memory Bandwidth | 2.0 TB/s | 3.35 TB/s | 8.0 TB/s |
| NVLink Version | 3.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| NVLink Bandwidth | 600 GB/s | 900 GB/s | 1800 GB/s |
| FP8 Performance | — | 4 PFLOPS | 10 PFLOPS |
| FP4 Performance | — | — | 20 PFLOPS |
| TDP | 400W | 700W | 1000W |
Key Observations:
1. Transistor Count: 2.6x increase from Hopper, enabled by dual-die design
2. Memory Bandwidth: 2.4x improvement through HBM3e
3. L2 Cache: 3.8x increase for better weight caching
4. NVLink Bandwidth: 2x improvement for multi-GPU scaling
5. Tensor Performance: 2.5x in FP8, 4x+ with new FP4 capability
6. New TMEM: Revolutionary addition solving register pressure
Real-World Performance Analysis
Let’s examine Blackwell’s performance in actual AI workloads:
Large Language Model (LLM) Training:
GPT-style models with 175B parameters:
• Hopper H100: ~420 TFLOPS effective (FP8)
• Blackwell B200: ~950 TFLOPS effective (FP8)
• Speedup: 2.26x
Trillion-parameter models:
• Requires model parallelism across multiple GPUs
• NVLink bandwidth becomes critical bottleneck
• Blackwell’s 2x NVLink improvement delivers near-linear scaling
• GB200 NVL72 enables 30x speedup over Hopper for trillion-parameter models
LLM Inference:
With FP4 quantization:
• 4x higher throughput vs FP8 (Hopper)
• 2x larger models fit in same memory
• Sub-millisecond latency for interactive applications
• Real-time trillion-parameter model inference becomes practical
The Transformer Engine’s micro-tensor scaling maintains >99.5% accuracy even at FP4, making this aggressive quantization viable for production deployment.
Computer Vision Workloads:
Image classification (ResNet-50):
• Limited by memory bandwidth, not compute
• Blackwell’s 8 TB/s HBM3e delivers 2.4x speedup
• Decompression engine adds 15–20% additional throughput
Object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN):
• Benefits from both compute and memory improvements
• 2.8x speedup on average
• Ray tracing cores accelerate certain detection algorithms
Scientific Computing:
Molecular dynamics (FP64):
• 45 TFLOPS FP64 tensor performance (B200)
• 13% improvement over Hopper
• Massive memory capacity enables larger simulations
Weather modeling:
• Memory bandwidth critical
• Large L2 cache improves temporal locality
• 2.2x speedup in typical weather simulation kernels
Software Ecosystem and Programming Model
Blackwell maintains full software compatibility with previous NVIDIA architectures while introducing new capabilities:
CUDA 12.x Support:
• Full backward compatibility with existing CUDA code
• New intrinsics for TMEM access
• Enhanced warp-level primitives for tensor operations
• Improved compiler optimizations for dual-die architecture
CUTLASS 3.x:
• Optimized GEMM templates leveraging TMEM
• 50x performance improvement in hand-tuned kernels
• Automatic selection of optimal tile sizes
• Support for all precision formats (FP4/FP6/FP8/FP16/TF32/FP64)
TensorRT-LLM:
• Automatic model optimization for Blackwell
• FP4 quantization with micro-tensor scaling
• Multi-GPU inference optimization
• KV-cache optimization for transformer models
Framework Support:
• PyTorch: Native Blackwell acceleration through torch.compile
• TensorFlow: XLA optimizations for Blackwell
• JAX: Automatic multi-GPU scaling with NVLink
• Triton: New TMEM extensions for custom kernels
Programming TMEM:
TMEM introduces new programming challenges and opportunities. Unlike traditional shared memory, TMEM is specialized for tensor operations:
```python
# Pseudocode for TMEM-optimized GEMM
def gemm_with_tmem(A, B, C):
 # Load operands into TMEM
 tmem.load_async(A_tile, A_ptr)
 tmem.load_async(B_tile, B_ptr)
 
 # Tensor core operation using TMEM
 mma.sync(C_tile, A_tile, B_tile) # Direct TMEM access
 
 # Write results back
 tmem.store(C_ptr, C_tile)
```
This eliminates the traditional shared memory staging, register allocation, and synchronization steps.
Competitive Landscape
Blackwell doesn’t exist in a vacuum. Let’s examine how it compares to competing AI accelerators:
vs AMD MI300X:
• MI300X: 192 GB HBM3, 5.3 TB/s bandwidth
• Blackwell B200: 192 GB HBM3e, 8 TB/s bandwidth
• Advantage: Blackwell’s 51% higher memory bandwidth and superior tensor performance
• MI300X unified memory architecture is competitive but lacks TMEM equivalent
vs Google TPU v5e/v5p:
• TPU v5p: Optimized specifically for transformer models
• Excellent performance for pure transformer workloads
• Blackwell more versatile across diverse workloads
• NVLink 5.0 offers superior multi-chip scaling compared to TPU interconnect
vs Intel Gaudi 3:
• Gaudi 3: 128 GB HBM2e, competitive pricing
• Lower performance than Blackwell in FP8/FP4 operations
• Different optimization philosophy (cost vs performance)
• Smaller software ecosystem compared to CUDA
vs AWS Trainium2:
• Purpose-built for LLM training
• Competitive inference performance
• Blackwell’s versatility advantage
• TMEM provides superior GEMM performance
NVIDIA’s Competitive Advantages:
1. Software Ecosystem: CUDA’s 15+ year head start is insurmountable
2. TMEM Innovation: Unique architectural feature with proven 50x benefits
3. Comprehensive Product Stack: From consumer RTX to datacenter GB200 NVL72
4. NVLink Technology: Best-in-class GPU-to-GPU interconnect
5. Transformer Engine: Hardware-software co-design for LLMs
Future Outlook and Architectural Evolution
Blackwell represents a major architectural shift, but it’s also a stepping stone to future innovations:
Post-Blackwell Predictions:
1. Increased Die Count: Three or four-die configurations could push beyond 500B transistors
2. Enhanced TMEM: Future generations might increase TMEM capacity to 512 KB or 1 MB per SM
3. Optical Interconnects: Moving beyond electrical NVLink to optical could enable 10+ TB/s per GPU
4. On-Package HBM: Integrating HBM dies directly on the GPU package could further reduce latency
5. Specialized AI Cores: Beyond tensor cores, dedicated attention mechanism accelerators could emerge
6. FP2 Precision: Even lower precision formats with advanced quantization techniques
Industry Impact:
Blackwell’s innovations will ripple through the industry:
• Trillion-parameter models become practical for production deployment
• Real-time inference for complex AI agents
• Multimodal models (vision + language + audio) in single-GPU systems
• Scientific computing acceleration enabling breakthrough discoveries
• Edge AI deployment with Blackwell-derived architectures
The dual-die approach, in particular, may become the standard for high-performance computing, as it elegantly solves the reticle limit problem while maintaining coherency.
Conclusion
NVIDIA’s Blackwell architecture represents the most significant leap in GPU design since the introduction of tensor cores in Volta. The dual-die configuration with 208 billion transistors, revolutionary Tensor Memory (TMEM), fifth-generation tensor cores supporting FP4 precision, and 192 MB L2 cache combine to create a system that’s not just incrementally better, but transformationally different.
Key architectural innovations:
1. TMEM solves the register pressure problem that has plagued tensor operations, enabling 50x performance improvements in optimized code
2. Dual-die design breaks through reticle limits while maintaining full cache coherency
3. FP4/FP6 precision with micro-tensor scaling doubles model capacity and performance
4. Massive 192 MB L2 cache dramatically reduces HBM traffic for weight-reuse workloads
5. NVLink 5.0 with 1.8 TB/s per GPU enables unprecedented multi-GPU scaling
6. 8 TB/s HBM3e bandwidth feeds the massive compute capabilities
Blackwell’s performance gains are staggering: 2–3x over Hopper in most workloads, with 30x speedups for trillion-parameter models in GB200 NVL72 configurations. But more importantly, Blackwell changes what’s possible: real-time inference on trillion-parameter models, practical deployment of multimodal AI, and breakthrough scientific computing applications.
The architecture’s influence extends beyond pure performance. TMEM’s success will likely inspire similar specialized memory structures in future accelerators. The dual-die approach demonstrates that multi-chip modules can achieve monolithic-like performance with proper interconnect design. And the aggressive push into FP4 precision shows that hardware-software co-design can unlock capabilities impossible through either approach alone.
For AI researchers and engineers, Blackwell represents a new baseline. Models that were previously impractical are now viable. Applications that required massive clusters can now run on smaller systems. And entirely new categories of AI workloads become possible.
NVIDIA’s Blackwell doesn’t just move the goalposts — it fundamentally redefines what we should expect from AI accelerators. The combination of architectural innovation, manufacturing excellence, and ecosystem integration creates a platform that will likely dominate AI computing for years to come.
The future of AI computing is here, and it’s built on 208 billion transistors of pure innovation.
